meta.title: nono
meta.subject: nono — capability-based sandboxing for AI agents

scenes.title: nono
scenes.tagline: OS-enforced sandboxing for untrusted AI agents

beats.narration: This is nono -- a capability-based sandboxing system.
Its purpose: run an untrusted A-I agent on your own machine, behind isolation the operating system itself enforces -- Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS.
It comes from the creator of Sigstore. We traced it through a Cargo workspace of four crates.
nono is a Cargo workspace of four crates -- and a single architectural conviction: keep mechanism and policy apart.
The library, nono, is a pure sandbox primitive. It ships with no security policy whatsoever. It applies exactly, and only, what a caller hands it.
Every judgment call -- which paths are dangerous, which commands to refuse -- lives one layer up, in nono-cli. The library is the mechanism; the C-L-I is the policy.
Two more crates complete the set. nono-proxy governs the network. And nono-f-f-i exposes the library to C through an auto-generated header -- so anything at all can embed it.

scenes.heading: The whole input, in one builder

beats.narration: This is the entire input to the nono library -- a CapabilitySet, assembled with a fluent builder. Nothing here is policy; it is a literal description of what will be permitted.
Grant read access to a project directory. Read-write to a single scratch file. Every capability is explicit, and nothing at all is allowed by default.
Set the network posture in one line -- block it outright, or force every connection through nono's auditing proxy.
Then a single call: Sandbox apply. The builder is the entire policy surface. After this line, enforcement no longer belongs to nono -- it belongs to the kernel.

scenes.heading: An untrusted run, start to finish

beats.narration: Here is a sandboxed run, start to finish. You ask the C-L-I to run an untrusted agent.
The C-L-I resolves your chosen policy into a concrete CapabilitySet, and hands it to the library's one function -- Sandbox apply.
Apply installs the rules into the kernel itself -- Landlock on Linux, Seatbelt on macOS -- and returns. From this point, confinement is the operating system's job.
Only now does the C-L-I exec the agent. It begins life already inside the sandbox -- there is no unconfined moment.
Say the agent reaches for something it should not -- your S-S-H keys, a credentials file, anything outside the capability set.
The kernel refuses. The syscall returns an ordinary permission error -- the very same one a missing file gives. The agent cannot tell it was sandboxed, and cannot reason its way around it.
The run finishes, contained. Nothing outside the CapabilitySet was ever, for one instant, reachable.
When a capability set says proxy-only, the sandbox permits exactly one network destination: nono-proxy. Every byte the agent sends goes through it first.
The proxy filters by route. Allow this host; refuse that one. A sandboxed agent reaches only the endpoints you explicitly sanctioned.
It injects credentials. The agent asks to call an A-P-I; the proxy attaches the token on the way out. The secret is spent on the agent's behalf -- but the agent never sees it, so it cannot leak it.
And because the proxy terminates T-L-S, it can audit the actual content of encrypted traffic -- not merely its destination. The network stops being a blind spot.
Isolation contains a bad run, but it cannot reverse one. So nono also snapshots the filesystem.
Before the agent starts, the SnapshotManager records the working tree through a Merkle tree -- content-addressed, so only the blocks that genuinely change are ever written.
Changed blocks land in a deduplicated ObjectStore. An ExclusionFilter keeps build output and caches out of the snapshot entirely, so it stays small and fast.
If a run goes wrong, you roll back -- the working tree returned, byte for byte, to the instant before the agent ever touched it.

scenes.heading: nono, in four ideas
points: Mechanism and policy, split — the library applies, the CLI decides.
A CapabilitySet in; kernel-enforced isolation out.
A proxy that filters routes and spends credentials the agent never sees.
Merkle-tree snapshots make every run reversible.

beats.narration: So, nono in four ideas. Mechanism and policy held apart -- the library applies, the C-L-I decides.
A CapabilitySet goes in; kernel-enforced isolation comes out.
A proxy that filters every route, and spends credentials the agent itself never sees.
And Merkle-tree snapshots that make every run reversible. Confine the agent, govern its network, and keep an undo button. That is nono.
